coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic performance of graphene quantum dot incorporated UiO-66-NH2 composite assembled on plasma-treated membrane
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Safa, Saeed
Khajeh, Mostafa
Oveisi, Ali Reza
Azimirad, Rouhollal
Salehzadeh, Hamid
coredata.dc:description:
The UiO-66-NH2 and graphene quantum dot suspension were synthesized through hydrothermal and electrochemical methods, respectively. The prepared species were assembled on a non-woven polypropylene substrate (NW-PP). The obtained microstructure and the efficiency of the assembling methods were compared to each other. The results showed that plasma treatmentof NW-PP, as the pre-treating procedure, was the most liable strategy concerning the homogenous distribuation of MOF (mass loading percentage of ~19.5 wt% of UiO-66-NH2) and improved filtering performance. Next, the photocatalytic performance against methylene blue was increased through addition of graphene quantum dot (GQD) to the NW-PP. The photoluminescence results confirmed that the presence of GQDs effectively inhibited the recombination of photogenerated electron/hole pairs. In addition, the photocatalytic constant rate was increased up to ~three times in the presence of GQDs. Quenching tests proved that the photo-generated
O2 − played a major role in the photodegradation of the dye.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zirconium-based MOF
Metal-organic framework
Graphene quantum dot
Membrane
Photocatalysis
Organic dye
Degradation
Pollutant

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of GQDs, UiO-66-NH2, and non-woven polypropylene membranes (NW-PPs)

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2021 The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

item-info.ce:copyright: The Society of Powder Technology Japan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of the bare and plasma treated non-woven polypropylene based membranes.

SEM images of the (a) bare NW-PP, (b) MOF/NW-PP, (c) MOF/NW-PP (CTAB associated deposition) and (d) MOF/plasma treated NW-PP. (e) The schematic view of the hydrogen bonding between hydroxyl and amine functional groups. (f) XRD spectra of MOF/NW-PP (CTAB associated deposition) and MOF/plasma treated NW-PP.

Raman (a) and FT-IR (b) spectra of the bare and GQDs incorporated UiO-66-NH2. The PL spectrum with deconvoluted peaks of the bare (c) and GQDs incorporated (d) UiO-66-NH2. (e) The EIS Nyquist plots of the bare and composite samples.

Photocatalytic performance of the bare and GQDs incorporated UiO-66-NH2/NW-PP samples. The inset attributed to the first-degree exponential fitting curve for calculation of photocatalytic constant rate (a). The effects of scavengers on photodegradation of dye (b). The results of membrane efficiency test (flux and retention ratio) of the bare and MOF deposited samples (c).

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic performance of graphene quantum dot incorporated UiO-66-NH2 composite assembled on plasma-treated membrane

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, University of Zabol, Zabol, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The UiO-66-NH2 and graphene quantum dot suspension were synthesized through hydrothermal and electrochemical methods, respectively. The prepared species were assembled on a non-woven polypropylene substrate (NW-PP). The obtained microstructure and the efficiency of the assembling methods were compared to each other. The results showed that plasma treatmentof NW-PP, as the pre-treating procedure, was the most liable strategy concerning the homogenous distribuation of MOF (mass loading percentage of ~19.5 wt% of UiO-66-NH2) and improved filtering performance. Next, the photocatalytic performance against methylene blue was increased through addition of graphene quantum dot (GQD) to the NW-PP. The photoluminescence results confirmed that the presence of GQDs effectively inhibited the recombination of photogenerated electron/hole pairs. In addition, the photocatalytic constant rate was increased up to ~three times in the presence of GQDs. Quenching tests proved that the photo-generated
O2 − played a major role in the photodegradation of the dye.

ce:section.ce:para: The newly developed metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) structures have been greatly concerned as liable candidates to be applied in the fields needing porosity and/or photocatalytic performance such as wastewater filtering and purification [1,2], photocatalytic hydrogen production [3], and water splitting [4]. The multi-functional property was firstly observed and described by by Xamena et al. [5]. The photocatalytic mechanism of MOFs is based on the photoexcited-electron injection from surrounding ligands to metal oxide clusters and subsequent well-known formation of decomposer radicals [6–8].

Among various MOFs, the (photo) chemical stability of UiO-66 makes it as a suitable candidate for self-cleaning filters [9–11]. However, compared to inorganic photocatalysts like TiO2 and ZnO, the efficiency of UiO-66 is low especially due to the fast charge recombination [6] and should be upgraded. Therefore, to increase the light-harvesting capability, some methods have been evaluated which are commonly based on addition of functional groups [10], or charges recombination inhibition through incorporation of electron acceptors beside MOF [12,13]. Among these methods, the incorporation of carbonaceous nano-species could cover both aims simultaneously [14]. For example, Lin et al. [12], observed that the incorporation of a little amount of rGO (1 wt%) to UiO-66/CdS composite could increase the photoassisted hydrogen production up to ~23%. More recently, Song et al. [15], reported that the graphene incorporated UiO-66-NH2 resulted in an ultrafast photothermal activity against dimethyl 4-nitrophenyl phosphate (~twice better than bare UiO-66-NH2).

On the other hand, aiming to usage of a photocatalytic filter, it must be tightly assembled on the surface of a polymeric membrane. This subject is understudying [16–18], due to inherent low reactivity of polymeric membranes. Recently, Lee et al. [18], suggested an effective way to overcome this problem and used an interface layer for self-assembly precipitation of UiO-66 and UiO-66-NH2. In fact, they showed that the hydroxyl groups on the ZnO thin film pre-coated on the non-woven polypropylene membrane (NW-PP), interact (through formation of hydrogen bonds) with the amine functional groups (on the surface of UiO-66-NH2). By using this procedure, they could to mount up to 30 wt% of MOF to NW-PP. Nevertheless, this protocol suffers from needing a time-consuming and expensive stage of atomic layer deposition of ZnO.

So, herein, we introduce an industrial-scale and fast method for preparation of NW-PP substrate by using an air plasma treatment for constricted sedimentation of our MOF. Furthermore, concerning the bare MOF photocatalytic inadequacies, we added graphene/graphene oxide in the state of quantum dots (QDs) on the surface of deposited MOFs. In fact, graphene quantum dot, not only has better capability for homogenous distribution among photocatalyst material, but also roles as an effective acceptor beside photocatalyst material [13] and that’s the main objective of this study.

All of the precursors were in analytical grade. The high purity graphite (widely using as heating element) was employed as the electrode for electrochemical synthesis of GQD. The NW-PP membrane was used as the substrate.

The GQD suspension was synthesized according to the method developed by Li et al. [19]. In summary, the completely pre-washed graphitic plate electrode (10 × 10 × 2 mm3) were fixed on a purpose-built holder at distance of 20 mm and put in an electrolyte (composed of 380 mg of NaOH, 1 mL DI water and 20 mL of ethanol). The electrodes were powered with 25 V-DC for 40 min. Afterwards, the darkened solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 4 h to separate the GQDs from thicker graphitic species.

For the synthesis of UiO-66-NH2 samples, the protocol reported by Katz et al. [20] was used. At first, 0.54 mmol of ZrCl4 was added to 1 mL of HCl and ultrasonicated up to complete dissolution. Afterward, 5 mL of N,Nʹ-dimethylformamide (DMF) was added to the glass vial and ultrasonicated for 15 min. Moreover, 0.75 mmol of 2-aminoterephthalic acid (BDC 1
1 1,4-Benzenedicarboxylic acid (Terephthalic acid). -NH2) was added to the prepared solution and once again, 10 mL of DMF was dripped to the solution, then the vial capped and sonicated for 10 min. In order to crystalize the Zr6O4(OH)4(BDC-NH2)6 nanostructure, the supersaturated solution was aged at 80 °C for 12 h. The yellowish white suspension was filtered and subsequently washed many times with DMF and ethanol, respectively. After that, the residual DMF was entirely vaporized by keeping the powder at 120 °C.

The last stage of experiment is how to apply MOF powder on a piece of NW-PP membrane. The main object was to fabricate a photocatalyst filter which not only traps the impurities inside its porosities but also degrade them to small molecules under light irradiation. The MOF powder was assembled on the purpose-treated NW-PP substrates by operating two main routes. Firstly, the surfactant CTAB (cetyltrimethylammonium bromide) was applied as the coupling agent between MOF and NW-PP [18]. Concerning this route, supramolecular complexes containing 0.04 g of CTAB and 0.24 g of UiO-66-NH2 in 200 mL of DMF were prepared. A piece of NW-PP (40 × 40 cm2) was immersed in each solution and stirred for 24 h in room temperature.

In the second route, NW-PP was pretreated under air-atmospheric plasma (50 kV) [21] to cover its surface with various hydrophilic agents. Subsequently, similar procedure without CTAB agent was applied for MOF assembly on the treated NW-PP.

The resulting MOF-integrated NW-PP samples were rinsed with DMF and then ethanol to thoroughly detach the weak-bonded and free particles. Finally, the ultimate products were dried in oven at 50 °C for 1 h and kept in a desiccator. The schematic of the synthesis procedure is summarized and included in the subsequent section (see figure SI-1)

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, VEGA TESCAN II) was used to depict the morphology of the prepared samples. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of samples were applied to study the surface functional groups by using a Shimadzu-8400S spectrometer in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 with KBr pellets. In addition, Raman spectroscopy at excitation wavelength of 785 nm by using a SENTERRA (Bruker) was used to evaluate the presence and effects of graphene quantum dots in the samples. Aiming to study the electronic interactions between MOF and GQDs, photoluminescence spectroscopy was performed by using a Perkin-Elmer LS-55.

For electrochemical measurements, the bare and composite powders were deposited on FTO conducting substrates by drop casting. After washing the FTO substrates, they were dryed by N2 flow. Afterwards, an insulating polyamide tape was used to obtain an exposed FTO area of 0.25 cm2 (0.5 cm × 0.5 cm). For preparing thin films, 6 mg of each sample was dispersed in 0.5 mL of acetone by sonicating for 10 min, and 10 μL of the obtained suspension was drop-casted on the exposed FTO area and dried at room temperature. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the samples were recorded on an EmState potentiostat (PalmSens Company connected to a PC by using PSTrace (4.8.7 version) software and a Gamry reference 600 workstations equipped with a PCI4/300 potentiostat in conjunction with EIS 300 software was used.

To assess the photocatalytic performance of the samples, the photocatalytic decolorization of methylene blue (MB) was carried out. In a typical process, the catalytic reaction was carried out in a 100 mL photoreactor, which contains 50 mL of MB dye (20 mgL−1) solution and a piece of catalyst loaded membrane. Irradiation was carried out using a combinatory UV and visible light by using a 400 W metal-halide light-source and photocatalytic performance was indirectly monitored by relating the optical absorbance at 664 nm to the degradation amount. The solutions were kept in dark for 1 h under magnetic stirring to reach the ads- and des-orption equilibrium state.

In addition, the dye nanofiltration of the samples was measured by using a purpose-built system. The membranes were fixed inside a tubular holder with an effective surface area of 1 cm2. The feed solution (40 ppm, methylene blue) was recycled by the plunger pump and the permeated solution was analyzed at intervals of 30 min. The decoloriztion of solution was analyzed using a spectrophotometer at 664 nm. The performance of the membrane was quantized by two main parameters as bellow [22]:
Flux = V / A · t · P
where V is the total volume of permeate, A is the effective surface area of membrane, t is the time interval and P is the applied pressure.
Rejection =
1 -
C
permeate
C
feed
× 100
where C is the concentration of solution measured by a UV–Visible spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs, UiO-66-NH2, and non-woven polypropylene membranes (NW-PPs)
ce:section.ce:para: The quality of the as-prepared graphene quantum dots was characterized by TEM image and photoluminescence spectra (see figure SI-2). The TEM micrograph shows the average size of nanoparticles lying in the range of 12–22 nm. To assess the major allotropic form of the carbon, the AFM topographical image was used as shown in figure SI-2c. The height of the particles on the surface of quartz substrate was <2 nm which belonged to the graphene quantum dots [23]. As shown in the photoluminescence spectra in figure SI-2, the extreme fluorescence should be considered in light-harvesting applications [23] which is due to its optical band gap (in vice versa of pristine graphene).

XRD pattern of MOF shown in figure SI-3a reveals that the synthesized UiO-66-NH2 powder via solvothermal method is well crystallized. The featured peaks of (111) and (200) agree quite well with the patterns of UiO-66-NH2. The other peaks are also consistent with the peaks reported in the literature [24–26]. The specific surface area is another important parameter to emphasis the porous nature of MOF that was about 1100 m2/g (see figure SI-3b).

As schematically shown in figure SI-1, the polypropylene based membranes are inherently superhydrophobic. In vice versa, the plasma treated NW-PP showed completely superhydrophilic property due to the presence of hydroxyl and/or other functional groups. In fact, the accelerated radicals inside plasma homogenously ionize the surface of NW-PP. In expose of hydroxyl groups, the surface would be relaxed by bonding with various functional groups [21]. The hydroxyl functional groups are appropriate and well-known sites for hydrogen bonding with MOF [18]. So as to have a better judgment on the density of hydroxyl groups of the bare and plasma treated NW-PP, the FTIR spectra (Fig. 1 ) was studied. As can be seen in that figure, by overlapping the polypropylene characteristic peaks, one can see that the typical hydroxyl-group related peak centered at ~ 3430 cm−1 is obviously strengthened. It has been reported that Plasma-formed radicals inducing by the abstraction of proton could initiate some radical reactions and chain backbone scissions resulting in creation of reactive site such as oxygen-containing –OH group (as shown by FTIR) on the PP surface [21].

The SEM images of the bare and deposited membranes are shown in Fig. 2 a–d. Concerning the MOF/NW-PP (Fig. 2b), a nearly sparse distribution of the particles is observed. This observation is ascribed to the lack of any activated sites on the surface of the bare NW-PP. Thus, MOF particles might be entrapped in the closed spaces during stirring.

As shown in Fig. 2c, the deposition is slightly improved by addition of CTAB during assembling. It should be noticed that bigger size accumulations are convened in the voids and closed spaces (during stirring) while the surface of the fibers seems to be naked, similar to that of Fig. 2b. Regarding this result, it can be concluded that cationic head of CTAB can adhere to electron-rich amine functional groups. Thus, the electrostatic repulsion of positive MOF crystals would be neutralized and the MOF particles can be connected to each other through van der Waals forces between CTAB tails to form bigger aggregations [18].

Although, the mass fraction of MOF seems to be qualitatively increased by addition of CTAB surfactant, the plasma treated sample revealed a very different condition for large and homogenous sedimentation of MOF (see Fig. 2d). The scheme shown in Fig. 2e reveals the suggesting mechanism for this result [18]. The interaction of embedded hydroxyl groups with the amine-groups of MOF through hydrogen bonding can lead to a well-controlled assembly of UiO-66-NH2 crystals on the surface of flexible non-woven tissue.

To assess crystallinity of UiO-66-NH2 after deposition, the XRD patterns of the two samples with higher MOF content were shown in Fig. 2f. The different intensity of the main peaks of MOF crystals confirms the larger mass loading of the plasma treated sample than the other [16].

The precise loading percentage of the samples was measured by using a 4 N analytical balance. The results show the mass loading percentage of 2.4, 5.1 and 19.5 wt% (of UiO-66-NH2 on the NW-PP substrate) for the MOF/NW-PP, MOF/NW-PP (CTAB associated deposition) and MOF/plasma treated NW-PP, respectively. The obtained results confirm the intriguing results of the SEM images. Although, our results are ~ 10 wt% lower than that of reported value by Lee et al. [18], however, having some advantages such as inexpensiveness, facile scalability and homogenous distribution make it an ideal choice for industrial scale application. In the next step, graphene quantum dot (GQD) was applied on the sample treated by plasma. The weight ratio of GQD to MOF was adjusted to be ≃ 1 wt%.

To confirm the presence of graphene quantum dot, Raman spectroscopy was used (Fig. 3 a). First of all, detection of Raman active mode of UiO-66-NH2 was carried out at excitation wavelength of 775 nm because of the presence of an unavoidable fluorescence at excitation wavelength of 532 nm [27]. The peak at ~ 493 cm−1 is related to vibrational modes of ZrO [28]. The peaks in the range of 800–850 cm−1 are typically due to OH bending [28]. The peaks around 1436–1456 cm−1 typically are assigned to OCO stretching in-phase [29]. The peak at 1632 cm−1 is assigned to C = C stretching of aromatic rings which is present in both MOF and GQD structure. Meanwhile, a broad peak at the range between 1100 and 1400 cm1- is appeared for the sample aggregated with GQDs. Rajender et al. [30] divided this convoluted zone to 6 distinct sub-peaks which are originated from the main defect-induced mode (D-band) of carbonaceous part (centered at ~ 1350 cm1-) in association with other functional groups and heterojunctions, as shown in the higher-magnified window shown in Fig. 3a. Meanwhile, the sp2 graphitized CC in the E2g vibrational mode peak is embedded with MOF characteristic peak at 1632 cm−1.

Additionally, FT-IR was chosen to evaluate the effects of GQDs on the functional groups of UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. 3b). As can be seen in there, various characteristic peaks of UiO-66-NH2 such as ZrO bond at ~ 400–650 cm−1, CO at ~ 1650 cm−1 and other well-known peaks are presented. The broad peaks in the range of 3050–3600 cm−1 are assigned to OH (adsorbed water and ZrOH) and NH stretching vibrations of primary amines in UiO-66-NH2. For the GQDs incorporated UiO-66-NH2, the peak shifts indicate the presence of hydrogen bond interactions between the primary amine groups and OH groups. In addition, some GQDs characteristic peaks at about 2900–3000 cm−1 are appeared [30]. Therefore, it seems that GQDs have made hydrogen bonds through NH2 functional group.

Another important issue relates to the active surface area of the samples. (see Fig. SI3b and c). It can be seen that the active surface area of the bare UiO-66-NH2 and UiO-66-NH2/GQDs composite is 1100 and 450 m2/g, respectively. Thus one can conclude that extremely small-sized GQDs are incorporated to the MOF porosities and probably resulted in appropriate and homogenous electronic interactions between GQDs and MOF structure.

It is known that UiO-66-NH2, despite having good visible light absorbance, suffers from severe fluorescence due to fast and extensive recombination of photogenerated charges which is not desirable for photoassisted applications [3,6,31,32]. In relation to this subject, photoluminescence spectra of the bare and the incorporated samples were evaluated, as shown in Fig. 3c and d. As shown in the Fig. 3, the deconvolution of the PL peaks revealed six main distinct sub-peaks.

To have a better judgment on the electron/hole recombination process, the decay-time of the UiO-66-NH2 was calculated through scheduled data acquisition in the phosphorescence mode (figure SI-4). Regarding a well-fitted first degree exponential equation on the results, the decay time was found to be ~37 μs, related to the main peak centered at ~445 nm. One should notice that this parameter is ~20–30 μs [33] for the titania nanowire, as the most commonly photocatalyst.

As expected, the photoluminescence peaks of the GQDs incorporated sample are attenuated (Fig. 3d). In fact, the large density of photogenerated electron/hole pairs might be separated around localized charge spaces formed by heterojunctions. This phenomenon inhibits the fast electron/hole recombination and forthcoming luminescence [14] and one can conclude that a considerable amount of electron/hole pairs would be existed.

In general, the reaction rate on the electrode surface is related to the arc radius of the Nyquist plots. The smaller the arc radius, the faster the interface charge transfer. As shown in Fig. 3e, the arc radius of the EIS Nyquist plot of the UiO-66-NH2/GQDs is smaller than that of the UiO-66-NH2-NH2 suggesting a relatively faster interfacial charge transfer for UiO-66-NH2/GQDs and subsequently, results in a more effective separation of photogenerated electron/hole pairs. This observation confirms the extinction of the photoluminescence peaks of the UiO-66-NH2/GQDs sample and is important concerning photocatalytic reactions.

After the successful characterization step, two application tests were designed and carried out, as shown in Fig. 4 . The results showed that the incorporation of GQDs effectively improved the photocatalytic performance of the bare NW-PP/MOF (Fig. 4a). The obtained data followed first-order exponential function and the constant rates were calculated to be 0.011 and 0.037 min−1 for the bare and GQDs incorporated MOF samples, respectively. These results are in good agreement with the results obtained from PL, indicating the importance of GQDs in the condition with the fast recovery of the membranes. It can be explained with the density of electron/hole pairs unconstrained due to charge space (around heterointerfaces) for making unconstrained electron/hole pairs [14]. Quenching tests were used to study active radical species leading to the degradation of dye [8,34]. Therefore, ethanol, p-benzoquinone, and EDTA-2Na were used as scavengers to trap the
OH,
O2 − and h+, respectively. Fig. 4b displays the change of photocatalytic performance upon adding these scavengers. As shown in the Fig. 4b, the addition of the ethanol, EDTA-2Na, and p-benzoquinone were decreased the degradation efficiencies from 98.6% to 97.5%, 85.9% and 57.3%, respectively. When using p-benzoquinone, the most obvious inhibition on photocatalytic was observed, indicating that
O2 − plays a major role in the photocatalytic degradation of dye and
OH and h+ play a minor role. Furthermore, the filtering performance of the membrane samples was measured. Fig. 4c shows the flux and rejection of a 40 ppm methylene blue solution among the prepared samples. Although, one expects a proportional decrement in the liquid transport flux due to the pathway volume contraction (by sedimentation of MOF on the fibers) [35,36], but it was unchanged by addition of low amount deposited samples (MOF/NW-PP, MOF/NW-PP (CTAB associated deposition)). It even was increased for the highly deposited sample (MOF/plasma treated NW-PP). The enhancement can be explained by the high intrinsic hydrophilicity of UiO-66-NH2 that facilitates the aqueous solution transport. It is noteworthy that this observation is very important for the industrial applications. Therefore, the UiO-66-NH2 decorated membrane is favorable additive for high flux filters.

The rejection performance of the samples was carried out by decolorization of a 40 ppm MB (as a well-known pollutant) aqueous solution. These results are shown in right Y-axis of the Fig. 4c. The compact structure of non-woven PP membrane has proportionally good filtration at relatively high flux. Meanwhile, after adhesion of UiO-66-NH2 nanopowder, the membrane showed a considerable increase in the retention ratio. In fact, the inherent microporosity of UiO-66-NH2 can serve as an microreactor for fast trapping and rapid physisorption of methylene blue molecules. Confirming this issue, the best rejection performance attained by the MOF/plasma treated NW-PP sample (which contains ~19.5 wt% of UiO-66-NH2) was 93% while other samples are lower than 60%.

This research reported a scalable and facile method for assembling of photocatalytic active MOF on a conventional membrane. The air-plasma treatment was a liable and facile method for structural modification of NW-PP substrate, as an appropriate host for photocatalysis. In this method, the surface of each fiber inside NW-PP functionalized by OH groups. A homogenously arranged UiO-66-NH2 (with high percentage of 19.5 wt%) was successfully deposited on the NW-PP substrate, as shown by SEM images and XRD patterns. Afterwards, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was incorporated on the deposited MOF. The results showed that the incorporation of graphene quantum dot in addition to UiO-66-NH2 considerably inhibits the recombination of photoassisted electron/hole pairs which resulted in the extinction of photoluminescence peaks. This subject was directly affected the photocatalytic performance of the samples. The photocatalytic constant rates for the MOF/plasma treated NW-PP and GQDs incorporated MOF/plasma treated NW-PP were obtained 0.011 and 0.037 min−1, respectively. Also, it was shown that sedimentation of MOF on the NW-PP substantially improved the filtering performance. The results showed that the UiO-66-NH2 MOF nanoparticles not only facilitate the transportation of the MB in aqueous solution (due to intrinsic hydrophilicity of MOF) among the fibers framework, but also improve the impurities entrapment. Thus, the retention ratio and flux rate were increased up to ~ 270% and 20%, respectively, by sedimentation of 19.5 wt% of MOF on NW-PP.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank from the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) for financial support (Number: 96011255).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apt.2021.02.013.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective adsorption of cationic dyes by UiO-66-NH2

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Chen, Q. He, M. Lv, Y. Xu, H. Yang, X. Liu, F. Wei, Selective adsorption of cationic dyes by UiO-66-NH2, Applied Surface Science, 327 (2015) 77-85.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient photodegradation of volatile organic compounds by iron-based metal-organic frameworks with high adsorption capacity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Li, S. Kim, J. Jin, H.C. Do, J.H. Park, Efficient photodegradation of volatile organic compounds by iron-based metal-organic frameworks with high adsorption capacity, Applied Catalysis B: Environmental, 263 (2020) 118284.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Calix[4]arene based dye-sensitized Pt@UiO-66-NH2 metal-organic framework for efficient visible-light photocatalytic hydrogen production

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-F. Chen, L.-L. Tan, J.-M. Liu, S. Qin, Z.-Q. Xie, J.-F. Huang, Y.-W. Xu, L.-M. Xiao, C.-Y. Su, Calix[4]arene based dye-sensitized Pt@UiO-66-NH2 metal-organic framework for efficient visible-light photocatalytic hydrogen production, Applied Catalysis B: Environmental, 206 (2017) 426-433.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancing visible-light photocatalytic activity for overall water splitting in UiO-66 by controlling metal node composition

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Melillo, M. Cabrero-Antonino, S. Navalón, M. Álvaro, B. Ferrer, H. García, Enhancing Visible-Light Photocatalytic Activity for Overall Water Splitting in UiO-66 by Controlling Metal Node Composition, Applied Catalysis B: Environmental, (2020) 119345.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: F.X. Llabrés i Xamena, A. Corma, H. Garcia, Applications for metal− organic frameworks (MOFs) as quantum dot semiconductors, J. Phys. Chem. C 111 (2007) 80–85.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hybrid BiOBr/UiO-66-NH2 composite with enhanced visible-light driven photocatalytic activity toward RhB dye degradation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Bibi, Q. Shen, L. Wei, D. Hao, N. Li, J. Zhou, Hybrid BiOBr/UiO-66-NH2 composite with enhanced visible-light driven photocatalytic activity toward RhB dye degradation, RSC Advances, 8 (2018) 2048-2058.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-Organic Framework (MOF) compounds: photocatalysts for redox reactions and solar fuel production
